critic_raw:
This RNN vs. statistical baseline for industrial equipment failure prediction has several fundamental methodological problems.

1. THE 7-DAY PRE-FAILURE LABEL WINDOW OVERLAPS WITH THE 7-DAY LOOKBACK WINDOW USED AS INPUT, CREATING LABEL-FEATURE TEMPORAL CIRCULARITY. The failure label is assigned to sequences occurring within 168 hours (7 days) before a logged failure. The RNN's lookback window is also 168 hours. This means that for a positive-labeled sequence, the lookback window spans exactly the 7-day period that defines the positive label. For the baseline logistic regression, the same 9 engineered features are averaged over the same 168-hour window. The features are derived from the same temporal window that defines the label -- if the sensor patterns that trigger labeling (degrading readings) also show up in the feature window, the model is partially predicting the label from its definitional signal rather than from predictive precursors.

2. STRATIFICATION BY MACHINE TYPE WITHIN A TEMPORAL SPLIT CANNOT PREVENT WITHIN-MACHINE-TYPE TEMPORAL SHIFTS. The split is temporal (months 1-24, 25-30, 31-36) and stratified by machine type. But individual machines within a type have their own maintenance histories, wear states, and failure modes that evolve over time. The machine-type stratification ensures all machine types appear in all splits, but it does not ensure that the specific machines in the test set are in comparable states to the machines in the training set. A machine that appears in all three splits but experienced a major maintenance intervention between months 24 and 25 will have a fundamentally different sensor baseline in the test period than in training -- yet this within-machine state change is invisible to the stratification.

3. F1 SCORE AS THE OPTIMIZATION TARGET AND PRIMARY METRIC IS SENSITIVE TO THE THRESHOLD USED TO BINARIZE PREDICTIONS. The validation set optimization and primary test evaluation both use F1 score. F1 is computed at a fixed decision threshold (implicitly 0.5 unless specified otherwise). The design does not specify the threshold at which F1 is computed. If the threshold is tuned on the validation set to maximize F1, this is a legitimate hyperparameter. If the threshold defaults to 0.5, this is an arbitrary choice that does not reflect the class imbalance in failure prediction (failures are rare, so the optimal threshold is typically well below 0.5). An AUC-first analysis would be threshold-free and more appropriate as the primary metric; F1 at the optimal precision-recall threshold should be secondary.

4. EQUAL-EFFORT TUNING OF LOGISTIC REGRESSION AND BIDIRECTIONAL LSTM IS IMPOSSIBLE TO OPERATIONALIZE FAIRLY. The design claims equivalent tuning effort for both models via grid search over regularization and learning rate. But bidirectional LSTM hyperparameter sensitivity (to learning rate, dropout, layer count, hidden units, sequence length) is qualitatively different from logistic regression hyperparameter sensitivity (to regularization strength alone). A grid search over a fixed compute budget that is adequate for logistic regression will not cover the same fraction of the LSTM's relevant hyperparameter space. The 'equivalent tuning effort' claim cannot be operationalized without specifying the number of grid points, compute time, and metric sensitivity for each model.

5. ROLLING FEATURE WINDOWS FIT ON TRAINING DATA BUT APPLIED AT THE BOUNDARY OF TEST SPLITS REQUIRE LOOK-BACK DATA FROM PRIOR SPLITS. The 24-hour rolling features (mean, std, min, max) are computed from a 24-hour window. For the first 24 hours of the test period (months 31-36), the rolling features require data from the last 24 hours of the validation period (month 30). If the test period is evaluated as a standalone segment without access to the preceding validation data for rolling feature computation, the first 24 hours of test data will have incorrect (e.g., zero-padded) rolling features. This is a standard edge effect in temporal sliding-window feature engineering that is not addressed in the design.
all_issues_raised: Label definition window and feature lookback window are identical (168 hours), creating circularity between predictive signal and label definition
Machine-type stratification does not account for within-machine temporal state changes from maintenance interventions
F1 score as primary metric is threshold-dependent and the threshold choice is not specified; default 0.5 is inappropriate under class imbalance
Equal tuning effort for LSTM and logistic regression is not operationally equivalent given their different hyperparameter space dimensionalities
Rolling feature windows at split boundaries require look-back data from prior splits that may not be available
7-day label window creates temporal label overlap for clustered failures; positive-class inflation in failure-dense periods not analyzed
Bidirectional LSTM incorporates future sensor readings in backward pass; look-ahead bias baked into architecture for prospective task
Lookback window equals label window; model learns to recognize already-in-failure-precursor sequences rather than predicting from normal-operation data
F1 as primary metric is threshold-dependent and unstable for rare-failure imbalance; threshold optimized on validation may not transfer to test
Machine age and maintenance history confounding not addressed by machine-type stratification; older machines may cluster in test period
168-hour label window and 168-hour RNN lookback window create asymmetric pre-failure signal across positive examples depending on position within the label window
Rare machine types may have zero failure events in validation or test windows, making per-machine-type F1 undefined and stratification-by-machine-type evaluation uninformative
Logistic regression baseline averages features over the window, discarding temporal dynamics that the RNN preserves — baseline is weaker than a non-averaged fixed-vector alternative
F1-optimized threshold on validation window may not hold on test window if failure rate or score distribution shifts temporally
Overlapping 168-hour sliding windows produce autocorrelated test instances, making bootstrap confidence intervals anti-conservative
